title: I debated a cardiologist on cholesterol *MUST WATCH*

chunks.title: Introduction to Cardiovascular Risk
chunks.summary: The hosts introduce the topic of cardiovascular risk and its relationship with LDL cholesterol and atherosclerosis. They emphasize the importance of understanding the various factors that contribute to cardiovascular disease.
chunks.transcript: Dr aloe thanks for coming on the podcast yeah thanks for having me I appreciate it we're both doctors and we're both just trying to help people understand how to reduce cardiovascular risk first

chunks.title: The Role of LDL Cholesterol
chunks.summary: Discussion centers around the established correlation between higher LDL cholesterol levels and increased risk of heart attacks, strokes, and cardiovascular diseases. The emphasis is on the findings from various studies that support this connection.
chunks.transcript: question would be how do you think about cardiovascular risk what causes atherosclerosis and how to modify it LDL cholesterol is causative of atherosclerosis so Brian ference said and his group said let's just look at every single study that's ever been done on cholesterol so they ended up with 20 million person years of follow-up and they found that without question no matter how you measure it the higher the LDL cholesterol the more likely you are to have atherosclerosis heart attack Strokes death mortality from

chunks.title: Insulin Resistance and Cardiovascular Disease
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to how insulin resistance alters the relationship between LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular disease. They highlight studies showing that individuals with high LDL but low insulin resistance have better outcomes.
chunks.transcript: cardiovascular disease and non-cardiovascular disease every study that has been done that lowers LDL cholesterol has definitely proven to have better outcomes than than not when I see the mainstream analyzes I see that and I think okay is it possible that there's another explanation for that that we're missing in the name of quote-unquote totality of the evidence and when I look at the details of LDL in connection with cardiovascular disease what I find is that there are many instances where LDL cholesterol does not associate with cardiovascular disease any study in which LDL cholesterol is stratified by a metric that correlates with insulin sensitivity or insulin resistance you see totally different amounts of correlation between LDL and cardiovascular disease when people are very insulin resistant versus minimally insulin resistant there are many many elderly studies in which people who are 80 85 Plus who have elevated LDL cholesterol tend to live the longest if you look at the amount of apob in a plaque that doesn't correlate with the amount of apob in the serum suggesting that there's no there's discordance there between able being the serum and able B in the plaque similarly if you correct LDL for LP little a levels then LDL corrected for LP little a is no longer predictive for cardiovascular disease it's only when the lp little a opponent of LDL is not accounted for that LDL is connected with cardiovascular disease and so we run

chunks.title: Misunderstanding LDL Correlation
chunks.summary: A critical perspective is presented regarding the reliance on LDL cholesterol as a causal factor for cardiovascular disease, suggesting that other factors such as metabolic health and insulin resistance may be more significant risk indicators.

chunks.title: Impact of Seed Oils on LDL Oxidation
chunks.summary: The impact of seed oils, particularly linoleic acid, on LDL oxidation is discussed. Evidence suggests that increased consumption of seed oils correlates with higher rates of LDL oxidation, which is harmful.

chunks.title: Saturated Fat vs. Seed Oils
chunks.summary: The hosts debate the effects of saturated fats versus seed oils on cardiovascular health, presenting studies that indicate saturated fats may be beneficial while seed oils might contribute to cardiovascular diseases.

chunks.title: Traditional Diets and Health Outcomes
chunks.summary: The conversation wraps up by exploring diet patterns from traditional cultures that are linked to lower rates of chronic diseases. Emphasis on nutrient-rich animal-based diets that have historically sustained populations with minimal health issues.
chunks.transcript: you say things like saturated fat clearly associated with cardiovascular disease and I can show you a huge meta-analysis of 6.5 million person years showing that saturated fat and dairy is actually potentially cardio protective it's to me this says you don't take a step back you take a step forward and you look at the details here I think we have to be better than this for our patients we have to think about these things and think okay there's a lot of conflicting evidence here what is the quality of each individual study what if humans done evolutionarily I think it helps give a framework of what we're talking about because what we've done evolutionarily is probably something that is reasonably good for humans people would say oh no no but if I look at any free living population of humans I consistently find massively lower rates of cardiovascular disease and chronic disease and diabetes they're essentially vanishingly rare so what are they doing differently than us they're eating meat they're eating organs a lot of them like the Messiah or the samburu they're milking cows they're eating dairy fat they're eating honey they're eating sugar they're not eating seed oils that's the thread that I pull on and go huh maybe we need to think about these things more carefully and I would assert that linoleic acid is potentially the worst thing that we could do for our patients if people are eating animal based they're eating animals they're eating meat they're eating organs things we've eaten have historic Leaf you're going to become insulin sensitive you're going to become more metabolically healthy you're going to get good nutrients and we don't need to fear LDL in the setting of metabolic health and that's my recommendations for people and I really appreciate you giving me the
